title: Celestial Signs: Orbits, Stars, Iron, and Light in the Quran
subtitle: Seven frequently cited Quran-and-science passages, read through classical tafsir and modern science with an honest strength assessment for each
summary: This article examines seven commonly cited Quran passages from the "scientific miracles" (i'jaz) literature — on orbiting celestial bodies, the oath by the stars' positions, mountains passing like clouds, paired plant life, water and life, iron "sent down," and the sun's versus the moon's light — giving the classical tafsir reading, the modern scientific gloss, and an honest assessment of each. It applies the site's caution framework throughout, showing that resonance ranges from strong to interpretively flexible to genuinely contested.

seo.metaTitle: Celestial Signs in the Quran: Orbits, Stars, Iron, and Light Assessed Honestly
seo.metaDescription: A verse-by-verse look at the Quran's celestial and scientific-sounding passages — orbits, star positions, mountains, plant pairs, water, iron, and light — with classical tafsir, modern science, and honest strength assessments.

sections.title: Introduction: Seven More Signs, One Framework
sections.body:
Alongside the more heavily debated passages on embryology, cosmology's "Big Bang" resonance, mountains as stabilizers, and the barrier between seas — each treated in its own dedicated article on this site — the Quran contains a further set of frequently cited nature verses: on the orbiting of celestial bodies, the oath by the "positions" of the stars, mountains "passing like clouds," paired plant life, water as the origin of living things, iron "sent down," and the differing terms used for the light of the sun and the moon. These verses circulate widely in popular Muslim apologetics (often called the i'jaz 'ilmi, or "scientific miraculousness," genre) as evidence that the Quran anticipated discoveries unavailable to a seventh-century audience.

This article treats each of the seven passages in turn, using the same caution framework laid out in "How to Approach Scientific Claims in Scripture Carefully": distinguishing scientific accuracy from prescience from theological meaning, insisting that a claim to prescience requires genuine temporal impossibility rather than mere compatibility, and taking seriously that words carry the semantic range available to their own language and era before they carry any modern one. For each verse this article gives the classical tafsir reading, the modern scientific gloss that popular apologetics builds on it, and an honest strength assessment along the three-tier scale used elsewhere on this site: strong resonance, interpretively flexible, or overclaimed/contested inference. None of these labels is a verdict on the Quran's truth as scripture; they are judgments about how much evidentiary weight a specific scientific argument can bear.

It is worth noting at the outset that the Quran itself typically frames these very passages as ayat — "signs" meant to provoke reflection (tafakkur) and gratitude in an already-believing or open audience, not as forensic proofs addressed to a skeptical scientific committee. Two of the passages discussed below end with exactly that language: Quran 13:3 closes, "Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought," and Quran 10:5 closes, "He details the signs for a people who know." Keeping that self-described purpose in view is not a way of lowering the bar for these verses; it is a way of applying the right bar.

sections.title: 1. "Each in an Orbit, Swimming": Quran 36:40 and 21:33
sections.body:
Quran 36:40 states that "it is not allowable for the sun to reach the moon, nor does the night overtake the day, but each, in an orbit (falak), is swimming (yasbahun)." Quran 21:33 makes the same claim more broadly: "it is He who created the night and the day and the sun and the moon; all [heavenly bodies] in an orbit are swimming."

Geocentric astronomy was dominant across late antique and medieval learned traditions, but it was not literally universal or intellectually static. A small number of ancient and medieval thinkers discussed Earth's rotation, and later Islamic astronomers critically revised Ptolemaic devices even while usually retaining geocentrism. The Quranic image of celestial bodies swimming in an orbit is compatible with observed regular motion, but it does not by itself choose between geocentric and heliocentric models.

The modern scientific gloss is considerably broader than what any seventh-century reader could have had in mind: not only do the sun and moon appear to move across the sky, but the Earth and every other planet genuinely orbit the sun under gravity, the moon orbits the Earth, and — a fact unavailable to any observer before twentieth-century astronomy — the sun itself orbits the center of the Milky Way galaxy, moving at roughly 220–230 kilometers per second and completing one full circuit (sometimes called a "galactic year") in something on the order of 225 to 250 million years. In that sense, "each in an orbit, swimming" describes the universe at every scale humans have so far been able to measure it, from moons to stars to the sun's own path through the galaxy.

A devotional resonance some later Muslim writers and preachers draw out — though it belongs to homiletics rather than to formal classical tafsir on these specific verses — is the parallel between the swimming or circling of the heavenly bodies and the tawaf, the ritual circumambulation of the Kaaba performed by pilgrims. On this reading, the orbiting of creation itself becomes a kind of cosmic worship, echoing the Quran's broader theme (as in 22:18 and 13:15) that all created things, willingly or unwillingly, submit and "prostrate" to God; the pilgrim who circles the Kaaba enacts in miniature what the sun and moon are imagined to enact on a cosmic scale.

The honest caveat is that the verses' own vocabulary is exactly as compatible with the old geocentric cosmology as with the modern heliocentric and galactic one — which is precisely why generations of readers before Copernicus took the same words as confirmation of an earth-centered universe, and readers after Copernicus and Hubble take them as confirmation of a sun-centered, then galaxy-embedded, universe. A form of words flexible enough to fit both pictures is functioning as a broad and lasting poetic truth about a universe full of motion, not as a specific prediction of any one physical model. **Assessment: strong resonance** with the general modern fact that virtually everything in the universe orbits something — tempered by the fact that the same wording was read, just as confidently, within an incompatible ancient cosmology for most of its interpretive history.

The Hebrew Bible offers its own poetic image of solar motion rather than a scientific claim. Psalm 19:4-6 describes the sun as being like "a bridegroom coming out of his room, like a strong man rejoicing to run his course," whose "going out is from the end of the heavens, his circuit to its ends." Read plainly, this too reflects the phenomenology of a sun that rises, crosses the sky, and returns to begin again — a literary circuit rather than a claim about orbital mechanics, and one that Biblical scholars generally read within the same observational, earth-centered framework common to the ancient Near East, rather than as an anticipation of heliocentric or galactic astronomy.

sections.title: 2. The Oath by the "Positions of the Stars": Quran 56:75-78
sections.body:
Quran 56:75-76 reads: "Then I swear by the setting of the stars (mawaqi' al-nujum) — and indeed, it is an oath, if you could know, [most] great." The passage continues in 56:77-78 to specify what the oath is actually about: "Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an, in a Register well-protected."

The Arabic mawaqi' is the plural of mawqi', from the root waqa'a, "to fall, to occur, to happen" — the same root that gives the Quran's vocabulary for the stars "falling" or "being scattered" at the Last Hour (as in 81:2). This is why classical and modern English translations of the phrase spread across a real semantic range rather than converging on one word: Saheeh International and Yusuf Ali both render it "the setting of the stars," Pickthall and Shakir render it "the places of the stars" and "the falling of stars" respectively. Classical exegetes offered several readings in the same spirit — the times and points on the horizon where stars rise and set, their fixed positions and courses across the sky, or (echoing 81:2) their scattering on the Day of Resurrection. The verse's own next line clarifies that its point is the greatness of the oath itself and, by extension, of what is being sworn upon (the Qur'an's protected transmission) — not a technical claim about stellar mechanics.

Modern popular apologetics nonetheless finds real scientific resonance in the idea of stellar "positions." Two facts are genuinely striking, if read into the term: first, because light takes a finite time to travel, the position at which we observe a distant star is never its current position — for the nearest stars this lag is a few years, for others many millennia, so that naked-eye and telescopic astronomy of "the positions of the stars" is, by necessity, always an exercise in observing where a star used to be. Second, stars in orbit — whether members of binary systems or, like the sun, orbiting a galactic center — are, in the language of general relativity, in a state of continuous free fall, following the curvature of spacetime rather than being pulled by a classical "force"; their apparent fixed "position" is itself the outcome of constant motion. Both of these are genuinely profound modern facts about what a stellar "position" even means.

The honest assessment has to weigh this resonance against how open-ended the term mawaqi' is and against what the passage's own continuation identifies as the actual point of the oath. An oath phrased broadly enough to be filled with almost any subsequently discovered fact about stars — their light-travel-time delay, their orbital free-fall, their gravitational dynamics, or simply their rising and setting — is exactly the kind of alignment the caution framework warns against treating as prediction: compatibility is not the same as impossibility-to-have-known. **Assessment: evocative resonance rather than a scientific prediction** — the linguistic openness of mawaqi' is what allows it to be filled with whatever modern astronomy an interpreter already has in mind.

Job 38:31-33 offers a striking Biblical parallel in register, if not in claim: God challenges Job, "Can you bind the cluster of the Pleiades, or loosen the cords of Orion? Can you lead the constellations out in their season? ... Do you know the laws of the heavens?" Both passages use the stars rhetorically, to humble the listener's claim to knowledge before the vastness of the sky, rather than to transmit specific astronomical data. Neither text is best read as a scientific claim about stellar positions; both use the stars as a rhetorical figure for what lies beyond ordinary human comprehension.

sections.title: 3. Mountains "Passing Like Clouds": Quran 27:88
sections.body:
Quran 27:88 reads: "And you see the mountains, thinking them rigid, while they will pass as the passing of clouds. [It is] the work of Allah, who perfected all things. Indeed, He is Acquainted with that which you do."

The verse's own immediate context is unambiguous about its setting. The verse before it, 27:87, describes the Day the Horn (Trumpet) will be blown, when "whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will be terrified except whom Allah wills," and the two verses after it, 27:89-90, describe the reward of "whoever comes [at Judgement] with a good deed" and the punishment of whoever comes with an evil one. Mainstream classical tafsir — al-Tabari, Ibn Kathir, and al-Qurtubi among them — accordingly reads the mountains "passing like clouds" as one more description of the cosmic upheaval of the Last Day, in the same register as other Quranic images of that day: mountains becoming "like fluffed-up wool" (101:5), removed and vanishing "as if it were a mirage" (78:20), or flattened into "a leveled plain" (20:105-106). On this reading, which is the plain and dominant sense of the verse in its own literary setting, the mountains that appear so solid to the living will be set into unimaginable motion at the Resurrection specifically — not a statement about ordinary geological process in the present age.

The modern popular-apologetic reading, prominent since the mid-to-late twentieth-century growth of the i'jaz 'ilmi genre, proposes instead that the verse describes plate tectonics: mountains, though rigid-looking, are in fact riding on tectonic plates that drift a few centimeters per year, so that over geological time they truly do "pass" like slow-moving clouds. Plate tectonics is itself a genuine, well-established twentieth-century scientific discovery. But applying it to this verse requires detaching "you see the mountains... while they pass" from the Day-of-Judgment frame that its immediately surrounding verses establish, and relocating it instead to describe the present geological era — a move the grammar and literary context do not themselves suggest, and one the classical exegetical tradition, working from the same Arabic text, did not make. This is a case where a mainstream classical reading exists, states plainly that the referent is the Last Day, and is simply set aside by the modern scientific reading rather than engaged with or refuted.

**Assessment: interpretively flexible at best.** The plate-tectonics gloss is a real reinterpretation of a verse whose own context, and whose classical tafsir, point elsewhere — toward eschatology rather than plate geology.

The Bible uses closely related imagery for exactly the eschatological purpose classical tafsir assigns to 27:88. Micah 1:4 describes divine judgment in similarly dramatic terms: "The mountains melt under him, and the valleys split apart, like wax before the fire, like waters that are poured down a steep place." No one reads Micah's melting mountains as an early description of volcanism or erosion; it is transparently apocalyptic imagery of judgment. The shared convention — mountains that appear permanent being dissolved, moved, or leveled as a sign of divine intervention at the end of history — is common currency across the ancient and late-antique Near East, which strengthens the case that Quran 27:88 belongs to that same genre rather than to a scientific description of the present geological era.

sections.title: 4. "Two Mates" Among the Fruits: Quran 13:3 and 36:36
sections.body:
Quran 13:3 describes God as the one "who spread the earth and placed therein firmly set mountains and rivers; and from all of the fruits He made therein two mates (zawjayn ithnayn)... Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought." Quran 36:36 states more broadly: "Exalted is He who created all pairs — from what the earth grows and from themselves and from that which they do not know."

Classical tafsir on these verses tends to read "pairs" broadly — as contrasting varieties within a single fruit (sweet and sour, light and dark, large and small), or simply as the general principle that God has built complementary duality into creation (as in the pairing of male and female humans, mentioned in the same verse, 36:36, alongside plants) — rather than as a specific technical claim about floral reproductive anatomy. The phrase in 36:36, "and from that which they do not know," is an open acknowledgment by the text itself that further categories of pairing exist beyond the audience's knowledge; later commentators and popular writers sometimes extend this to modern discoveries of paired phenomena in physics (such as particle-antiparticle pairs), though that is a homiletical extension well beyond the verse's plant-and-human context rather than a claim the classical tradition made about it.

Ancient Near Eastern reliefs are sometimes interpreted as showing artificial pollination of date palms, but the scenes may also carry ritual or royal symbolism and their precise action is debated. They can support the general point that people long understood practical differences between productive palms, but they should not be treated as an uncontested visual record of botanical theory without a specialist archaeological citation.

**Assessment: modest resonance, honestly qualified.** The Quran's "pairs" language touches a real botanical phenomenon that was already part of the practical, working knowledge of its first audience through date-palm cultivation; it does not itself state the general scientific principle of plant sexuality later established by early modern botany, and classical exegesis read "pairs" more broadly than reproductive biology alone.

sections.title: 5. "Every Living Thing" From Water: Quran 21:30
sections.body:
Quran 21:30 reads: "Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, and We separated them, and made from water every living thing? Then will they not believe?" This article's companion piece on cosmology treats the verse's first clause — the heavens and earth as an originally "joined entity" later "separated" — at length, including the contested resonance some readers find with modern cosmology's expanding, once-unified early universe; readers interested in that half of the verse should consult the "Cosmology: Creation and the Universe" article.

The clause relevant here — "We made from water every living thing" — states a biological dependency that modern science fully confirms: every known form of life, from single cells to complex organisms, requires water for its basic chemistry, and water remains the medium in which the biochemical reactions of life take place. In that narrow sense the claim is scientifically accurate and remains so today.

The honest caveat, consistent with the caution framework's emphasis on distinguishing accuracy from prescience, is that this was also intuitively available knowledge to the Quran's original desert-dwelling audience, and indeed to virtually every agrarian and pastoral society in history. Anyone who has watched crops wither without rain, watched livestock and travelers die of thirst in the desert, or seen an oasis sustain abundant life against a backdrop of lifeless sand has direct experiential grounds for concluding that water is what makes living things possible. Other ancient Near Eastern cultures reached similar conclusions through their own cosmologies — Egyptian civilization built its entire understanding of fertility and renewal around the life-giving Nile flood, and Mesopotamian myth placed the primordial waters (Apsu and Tiamat) at the origin of all things. This is a case, in the caution framework's terms, of accurate observation rather than prescient or otherwise inaccessible knowledge: it required no instrument, no theory, and no information unavailable to the text's own time and place to arrive at. **Assessment: accurate but not prescient** — true both to modern biology and to what careful observation had already taught desert and agrarian peoples for millennia.

Genesis 1:20-21 shows the same association of water with the origin of living creatures: "Let the waters abound with living creatures... God created the large sea creatures and every living creature that moves, with which the waters swarmed." Like the Quranic verse, this reflects the ancient and cross-culturally common recognition that water and life are inseparable; it is best read, on both sides, as theologically framed observation rather than as biochemical prescience.

sections.title: 6. Iron "Sent Down": Quran 57:25
sections.body:
Quran 57:25 reads, in relevant part: "...And We sent down (anzalna) iron, wherein is great military might and benefits for the people, and so that Allah may make evident those who support Him and His messengers unseen. Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might."

The modern scientific gloss draws on stellar nucleosynthesis: iron sits at the peak of nuclear binding energy, meaning it is the heaviest element that ordinary nuclear fusion inside the cores of massive stars can efficiently produce, with iron and heavier elements reaching interstellar space chiefly through the deaths of those stars in supernova explosions. In this precise sense, essentially all of Earth's iron — including the iron in its molten core and in every iron tool ever forged — is stellar in origin and predates the Earth itself, formed inside stars that lived and died before the solar nebula that became the Sun and its planets ever collapsed into being. Read with maximal literalness, "We sent down iron" is a striking match for the idea that iron genuinely arrived in the material that built Earth from beyond it, rather than originating here.

The linguistic counterpoint that the caution framework requires be weighed alongside this resonance is that anzala/nazzala ("to send down") is Quranic vocabulary used well beyond contexts of literal descent from the sky. Quran 39:6 uses the same verb for livestock: "...and He produced (anzala) for you from the grazing livestock eight mates..." — cattle plainly do not fall from the sky, so anzala here functions as "provided" or "brought into being for your benefit." Quran 7:26 uses the related anzalna for clothing: "We have sent down (anzalna) to you clothing to conceal your private parts and as adornment" — garments are manufactured by human hands from plant and animal fiber, not literally lowered from heaven. Classical Arabic usage and tafsir recognize this broader idiomatic sense of anzala as divine provision or bestowal, distinct from its more literal use for revelation and rain.

Two readings therefore genuinely compete for 57:25. A maximalist reading takes "sent down" as a literal, physically descriptive statement about iron's cosmic origin — a striking resonance with modern astrophysics if adopted. A linguistically conservative reading treats anzala here exactly as it functions for livestock and clothing elsewhere in the Quran: a general idiom for divine provision, making a claim about iron's usefulness to human civilization (metallurgy, tools, weaponry) rather than about its astrophysical origin at all. Because the same verb demonstrably carries the non-literal sense elsewhere in the same text, the inference to a literal cosmic claim cannot be treated as settled by the word choice alone. **Assessment: striking resonance, but a contested inference** — the astrophysics of iron's stellar origin is not in question; whether 57:25 is actually asserting it is.

It is worth noting, for comparative purposes, that the Bible frames iron in the opposite direction. Job 28:2, part of a poem about mining and human ingenuity, states plainly: "Iron is taken out of the earth, and copper is smelted out of the ore." Where the Quran's phrasing (on the maximalist reading) points outward, toward the heavens, for iron's origin, the Biblical text's plain sense points downward, toward the ground beneath human feet — describing the ordinary, observable experience of ancient mining and metallurgy rather than any claim about cosmic origins. Neither framing was making a claim about nucleosynthesis; the contrast simply illustrates how differently two ancient texts can describe the same metal depending on their immediate rhetorical purpose.

sections.title: 7. Lamp and Reflected Light: Quran 25:61, 71:16, and 10:5
sections.body:
Three passages consistently distinguish the vocabulary used for the sun from that used for the moon. Quran 25:61 describes God as the one "who has placed in the sky great stars and placed therein a [burning] lamp (siraj) and luminous moon (qamaran muniran)." Quran 71:16 states that God "made the moon therein a [reflected] light (nur) and made the sun a burning lamp (siraj)." Quran 10:5 states that God "made the sun a shining light (diya') and the moon a derived light (nur), and determined for it phases — that you may know the number of years and account [of time]." Across all three, the sun consistently receives the more intense vocabulary — siraj (blazing lamp) or diya' (radiant, burning light) — while the moon consistently receives nur or its related form munir (light, luminosity), a comparatively softer and more general term. Saheeh International's translation signals its own interpretive leaning by inserting the bracketed gloss "[reflected]" before "light" for the moon in 71:16.

Modern astronomy is not in doubt on the underlying physical fact: the sun generates its own light through nuclear fusion, while the moon has no light of its own and shines purely by reflecting sunlight off its surface. What is genuinely debated — including within the Islamic exegetical tradition itself, independent of any outside polemic — is whether the Quran's terminological distinction is best read as encoding that specific mechanism. Ibn Kathir's tafsir on 10:5 comments that God "made the rays that come forth from the bright sun as the source of light, and made the beams that come forth from the moon as light," made "of two different natures so they would not be confused with one another" — a real distinction, though stated without explicitly invoking reflection as a physical mechanism. The twentieth-century Ma'arif-ul-Qur'an commentary of Mufti Muhammad Shafi goes further in urging caution: it notes that some commentators propose diya' denotes intrinsic light and nur denotes light "derived from something else," but adds that "this semantic approach appears to be influenced by Greek thought, otherwise it has no lexical basis," and that "the Qur'an too has not given any categorical verdict about it" — preferring instead the more modest explanation that diya' simply denotes strong, work-enabling daylight while nur denotes general light of any intensity or source, a difference of degree and function rather than of physical mechanism.

A further honest caveat concerns how available this idea already was. Greek astronomers as early as Anaxagoras (fifth century BCE) had proposed that the moon shines by reflected sunlight, and the idea became a standard part of later Hellenistic astronomy, transmitted through late-antique Alexandria, Syriac Christian scholarship, and the Sasanian Persian court — all of which stood in cultural contact with the wider late-antique Near East, even if precisely what reached the Hijaz by the seventh century is difficult to establish with certainty. The insight was not, in other words, exotic or unreachable in principle for a late-antique audience.

**Assessment: a genuine and consistent terminological distinction whose significance is debated even among the Quran's own classical and modern commentators** — some read it as anticipating the sun/moon light difference science later confirmed in mechanism; others, working from the same Arabic text, caution that the vocabulary supports only a difference of intensity and function, not a claim about reflection as such.

By contrast, Genesis 1:16 makes no terminological distinction at all between the two bodies' light: "God made the two great lights: the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night." Both sun and moon are simply "lights" (the Hebrew me'orot), differentiated only by relative size and by day/night function, not by any linguistic marker of intrinsic versus derived luminosity. This is a useful comparative data point: it shows that a text can describe the sun and moon accurately and reverently, as Genesis does, without drawing the terminological distinction the Quran draws — which means the Quran's distinction, whatever its ultimate significance, is not the only way an ancient Near Eastern scripture handled the topic.

sections.title: Applying the Framework: Signs, Not a Physics Textbook
sections.body:
Laid side by side, these seven passages resist a single verdict, which is itself the honest finding: the orbiting of celestial bodies is a strong resonance whose wording is equally at home in the geocentric cosmology it was first read within; the "positions of the stars" is evocative but linguistically open enough to fit whatever astronomy a reader already holds; mountains "passing like clouds" carries a plain classical meaning about the Day of Judgment that the plate-tectonics reading sets aside rather than builds on; paired fruits touch a real but only partially known ancient practice; water as life's origin is accurate but was already available to unaided observation; iron "sent down" rests on a striking astrophysical fact paired with a live linguistic counter-reading from the Quran's own usage elsewhere; and the sun/moon light distinction is real in the text but its significance is contested by the very tafsir tradition that transmits it.

What unites the two verses that close with an explicit statement of purpose — 13:3's "signs for a people who give thought" and 10:5's "He details the signs for a people who know" — is that the Quran's own stated aim for this material is ayat: prompts toward reflection, gratitude, and recognition of a Creator, addressed most directly to an audience already inclined to look for them. That is a theological and rhetorical purpose, not a scientific one, and the distinction matters in both directions. It means these verses should not be dismissed as failures simply because they do not read like a modern astronomy or geology textbook — that was never their genre or aim. But it equally means that treating them as forensic scientific predictions, capable of settling debates about the Quran's origin, asks them to bear a kind of evidentiary weight their own context does not claim for them. As the companion article on approaching scientific claims carefully argues, overclaiming here does not strengthen the religious case being made for these verses; by inviting exactly the scrutiny under which alignment collapses into either geocentric-compatible poetry, contested linguistics, or knowledge the original audience already possessed, it can weaken it. The more durable claim — available whether or not any given scientific resonance holds up — is the text's own: that a reflective observer, looking at orbiting bodies, a star-strewn sky, standing mountains, fruit-bearing plants, water-dependent life, useful metal, and the different lights of day and night, might reasonably be moved to wonder about their maker.

quranVerses.arabic: لَا ٱلشَّمْسُ يَنۢبَغِى لَهَآ أَن تُدْرِكَ ٱلْقَمَرَ وَلَا ٱلَّيْلُ سَابِقُ ٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَكُلٌّۭ فِى فَلَكٍۢ يَسْبَحُونَ
quranVerses.translation: It is not allowable for the sun to reach the moon, nor does the night overtake the day, but each, in an orbit, is swimming.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّۭ فِى فَلَكٍۢ يَسْبَحُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And it is He who created the night and the day and the sun and the moon; all [heavenly bodies] in an orbit are swimming.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: Then I swear by the setting of the stars,
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: And indeed, it is an oath - if you could know - [most] great.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation
api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَتَرَى ٱلْجِبَالَ تَحْسَبُهَا جَامِدَةًۭ وَهِىَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ ٱلسَّحَابِ ۚ صُنْعَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَتْقَنَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَفْعَلُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And you see the mountains, thinking them rigid, while they will pass as the passing of clouds. [It is] the work of Allah, who perfected all things. Indeed, He is Acquainted with that which you do.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى مَدَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنْهَٰرًۭا ۖ وَمِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ جَعَلَ فِيهَا زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۖ يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَٰتٍۢ لِّقَوْمٍۢ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And it is He who spread the earth and placed therein firmly set mountains and rivers; and from all of the fruits He made therein two mates; He causes the night to cover the day. Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: سُبْحَٰنَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَمِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَمِمَّا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Exalted is He who created all pairs - from what the earth grows and from themselves and from that which they do not know.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًۭا فَفَتَقْنَٰهُمَا ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ حَىٍّ ۖ أَفَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, and We separated them and made from water every living thing? Then will they not believe?
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ وَأَنزَلْنَا مَعَهُمُ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ لِيَقُومَ ٱلنَّاسُ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَأَنزَلْنَا ٱلْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌۭ شَدِيدٌۭ وَمَنَٰفِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥ وَرُسُلَهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: We have already sent Our messengers with clear evidences and sent down with them the Scripture and the balance that the people may maintain [their affairs] in justice. And We sent down iron, wherein is great military might and benefits for the people, and so that Allah may make evident those who support Him and His messengers unseen. Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍۢ وَٰحِدَةٍۢ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَنْعَٰمِ ثَمَٰنِيَةَ أَزْوَٰجٍۢ ۚ يَخْلُقُكُمْ فِى بُطُونِ أُمَّهَٰتِكُمْ خَلْقًۭا مِّنۢ بَعْدِ خَلْقٍۢ فِى ظُلُمَٰتٍۢ ثَلَٰثٍۢ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ۖ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُصْرَفُونَ
quranVerses.translation: He created you from one soul. Then He made from it its mate, and He produced for you from the grazing livestock eight mates. He creates you in the wombs of your mothers, creation after creation, within three darknesses. That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs dominion. There is no deity except Him, so how are you averted?
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: يَٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًۭا يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَٰتِكُمْ وَرِيشًۭا ۖ وَلِبَاسُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌۭ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ
quranVerses.translation: O children of Adam, We have bestowed upon you clothing to conceal your private parts and as adornment. But the clothing of righteousness - that is best. That is from the signs of Allah that perhaps they will remember.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ بُرُوجًۭا وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا سِرَٰجًۭا وَقَمَرًۭا مُّنِيرًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Blessed is He who has placed in the sky great stars and placed therein a [burning] lamp and luminous moon.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَجَعَلَ ٱلْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُورًۭا وَجَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ سِرَاجًۭا
quranVerses.translation: And made the moon therein a [reflected] light and made the sun a burning lamp?
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ ضِيَآءًۭ وَٱلْقَمَرَ نُورًۭا وَقَدَّرَهُۥ مَنَازِلَ لِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ ۚ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ذَٰلِكَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ لِقَوْمٍۢ يَعْلَمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: It is He who made the sun a shining light and the moon a derived light and determined for it phases - that you may know the number of years and account [of time]. Allah has not created this except in truth. He details the signs for a people who know
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: Their voice has gone out through all the earth, their words to the end of the world. In them he has set a tent for the sun, which is as a bridegroom coming out of his room, like a strong man rejoicing to run his course. His going out is from the end of the heavens, his circuit to its ends; There is nothing hidden from its heat.

“Can you bind the cluster of the Pleiades, or loosen the cords of Orion? Can you lead the constellations out in their season? Or can you guide the Bear with her cubs? Do you know the laws of the heavens? Can you establish its dominion over the earth?
The mountains melt under him, and the valleys split apart, like wax before the fire, like waters that are poured down a steep place.

God said, “Let the waters abound with living creatures, and let birds fly above the earth in the open expanse of the sky.” God created the large sea creatures and every living creature that moves, with which the waters swarmed, after their kind, and every winged bird after its kind. God saw that it was good.
Iron is taken out of the earth, and copper is smelted out of the ore.
God made the two great lights: the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night. He also made the stars.

faq.question: Does the Quran's description of celestial bodies "orbiting" match modern astronomy?
faq.answer: The core claim — that the sun, moon, and other bodies move continuously along a path ("each in an orbit, swimming," Quran 36:40 and 21:33) — resonates strongly with the modern fact that essentially everything in the universe, from moons to planets to stars orbiting galactic centers, is in orbital motion. The important caveat is that this same wording was read for roughly a thousand years within a geocentric cosmology, in which the sun and moon were understood to orbit a fixed Earth. The verse's language is broad enough to fit either picture, which is exactly why it does not, on its own, function as a specific prediction of heliocentrism or galactic astronomy.

faq.question: What does "the positions of the stars" in Quran 56:75-76 actually mean?
faq.answer: The Arabic mawaqi' comes from a root meaning "to fall" or "to occur," and reputable English translations render it variously as "the setting of the stars" (Saheeh International, Yusuf Ali), "the places of the stars" (Pickthall), or "the falling of stars" (Shakir). Classical exegetes read it as referring to stars' rising/setting points, their general positions, or their scattering at the Last Hour. The verse's own continuation (56:77-78) identifies the point of the oath as the protected transmission of the Qur'an, not a technical claim about stellar mechanics.

faq.question: Is Quran 27:88 ("mountains pass like clouds") about plate tectonics or about the Day of Judgment?
faq.answer: Mainstream classical tafsir — including al-Tabari, Ibn Kathir, and al-Qurtubi — reads the verse squarely within its immediate context: the verse before it (27:87) describes the Trumpet being blown, and the verses after it (27:89-90) describe reward and punishment "at Judgement." The classical reading takes the mountains' motion as part of the cosmic upheaval of the Last Day. The plate-tectonics reading, popular in modern apologetics, is a later reinterpretation that sets aside this eschatological context rather than building on it; it should be understood as an interpretively flexible modern gloss, not the verse's established classical meaning.

faq.question: Did people in the seventh century already know that plants have male and female forms?
faq.answer: Partially, and practically rather than systematically. The date palm — central to the economy of the Hijaz — is dioecious (individual trees are male or female) and requires hand-pollination, a practice already old by the seventh century (attested in Assyrian reliefs from the ninth century BCE and in Theophrastus's writings, and reflected in a hadith in Sahih Muslim, no. 2363, about the Prophet's companions pollinating palms in Medina). What did not exist until the late seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries (with botanists like Nehemiah Grew and Rudolf Jakob Camerarius) was the general, systematic scientific principle that flowering plants as a class reproduce sexually through distinguishable male and female structures.

faq.question: Does the extraterrestrial origin of iron prove Quran 57:25 is a scientific miracle?
faq.answer: The astrophysics is solid: iron is produced by nuclear fusion in the cores of massive stars and reaches planet-forming material largely through supernova explosions, so Earth's iron does predate the Earth. Whether Quran 57:25's "sent down" is asserting this specific fact is contested, because the same verb (anzala) is used elsewhere in the Quran for livestock (39:6) and clothing (7:26), where it clearly means "provided" rather than "descended physically from the sky." Both a literal-cosmic reading and a "divine provision" idiom reading remain live options for the same word.

faq.question: Why does the Quran use different words for the light of the sun and the moon?
faq.answer: The sun is consistently called siraj (blazing lamp) or diya' (radiant light), while the moon is called nur or munir (light, luminosity) — a real and consistent terminological pattern across 25:61, 71:16, and 10:5. Modern astronomy confirms the underlying physical fact that the sun generates its own light while the moon only reflects it. Whether the Quran's word choice is meant to encode that specific mechanism is debated even within the Islamic exegetical tradition: Ibn Kathir treats the sun and moon as having "two different natures," while the modern Ma'arif-ul-Qur'an commentary cautions that reading "reflected versus intrinsic" into diya' and nur "has no lexical basis" and may reflect imported Greek philosophical categories rather than the Arabic language itself.

furtherReading.title: The Bible, the Qur'an and Science
furtherReading.author: Maurice Bucaille
furtherReading.note: The foundational and most widely circulated text of the modern "scientific miracles" (i'jaz) genre discussed throughout this article; hugely popular in Muslim apologetics, but its methodology has also drawn substantial scholarly critique from both Muslim and non-Muslim reviewers.

furtherReading.title: Islam's Quantum Question: Reconciling Muslim Tradition and Modern Science
furtherReading.author: Nidhal Guessoum
furtherReading.note: A critical assessment of the i'jaz 'ilmi movement by a Muslim astrophysicist, including a chapter-length evaluation of specific "scientific miracle" claims and their methodological weaknesses.

furtherReading.title: Tafsir Ibn Kathir (abridged English edition)
furtherReading.author: Ibn Kathir (trans. various)
furtherReading.note: The classical fourteenth-century commentary cited in this article for its reading of the sun/moon light distinction (10:5) and for the Day-of-Judgment context of Quran 27:88.

furtherReading.title: Ma'arif-ul-Qur'an
furtherReading.author: Mufti Muhammad Shafi (trans. Muhammad Hasan Askari and Muhammad Shamim, rev. Muhammad Taqi Usmani)
furtherReading.note: A widely used twentieth-century commentary cited in this article for its explicit caution against over-reading the diya'/nur distinction between the sun and moon as a claim about reflected versus intrinsic light.
